user_query:
I'm a novelist who writes dark, occult-tinged stories, and my late-night writing sessions have started to lag. I want a "midnight appraisal" of my machine that I can share with my critique group. Please turn the provided stub into a self-contained diagnostics tool and produce a concise system status report with an I/O benchmark that reads a thematically appropriate public-domain text.

Requirements and deliverables:
- Update the config file at config/diagnostics.yaml as follows:
  - Set tagline to: "A midnight appraisal of my writing lair."
  - Set thresholds.disk_warn_gb to 5 and thresholds.cpu_load_warn to 1.5.
  - Extend the distractors list by appending "twitter" and "reddit" (keep existing entries).
- Modify tools/session_diagnostics.py to implement a runnable script that:
  - Reads the updated config/diagnostics.yaml.
  - Downloads a public-domain plain text (UTF-8) copy of "The King in Yellow" by Robert W. Chambers from the official Project Gutenberg site (domain pattern: gutenberg.org) and saves it to external/king_in_yellow.txt. Reuse the file if already present.
  - Performs a simple I/O read test on external/king_in_yellow.txt: read the entire file sequentially enough times to measure elapsed time (e.g., 3 passes), compute the total bytes read, wall-clock read time in seconds, throughput in MB/s, and the SHA-256 hex digest of the file content. The file used for the test must be at least 100 KB.
  - Captures a system snapshot with at least:
    - cpu_count (from the current machine) and disk_free_gb for the current workspace directory.
    - load_avg_1m if available on this OS; else use null.
    - Top processes (up to 5) by memory use, including pid, name, and memory (MB) if obtainable; otherwise leave an empty list. Use portable approaches (e.g., ps on Unix or tasklist on Windows) as available.
  - Computes warnings based on the updated config:
    - Low disk space if disk_free_gb < thresholds.disk_warn_gb.
    - High 1-minute load if load_avg_1m is available and > thresholds.cpu_load_warn.
    - Distractor running: one warning per distractor term found (case-insensitive substring) in any listed process name.
  - Writes two outputs that must agree on shared values:
    1) output/system_report.json with fields:
       - version (string you choose),
       - source_label: "Project Gutenberg - The King in Yellow (plain text UTF-8)",
       - config: {tagline, thresholds, distractors},
       - metrics: {cpu_count, load_avg_1m or null, disk_free_gb},
       - io_test: {path: "external/king_in_yellow.txt", bytes_read, read_time_s, throughput_mb_s, sha256_hex},
       - top_processes: list of up to 5 objects {pid, name, rss_mb if available},
       - distractors_found: list of strings,
       - warnings: list of strings.
    2) output/system_report.md with sections: a title containing the tagline, "System Snapshot", "I/O Test (The King in Yellow)", "Top Processes", and "Warnings". The Markdown must include the same sha256_hex and bytes_read values found in the JSON.
- Draft a short, plain-text email to share with my beta readers summarizing the appraisal and save it to output/email_draft_to_beta_readers.txt. The draft must:
  - Include a Subject line containing the words "midnight appraisal".
  - In the body, mention the tagline, the current disk_free_gb and io_test throughput_mb_s values, the count of warnings (with a short bullet for each warning), and note that the I/O test used The King in Yellow from Project Gutenberg.

Constraints and notes:
- All paths are relative to the workspace. Do not hardcode numeric results; compute them at runtime.
- The Internet download is required; do not substitute a local file. If the Project Gutenberg file cannot be fetched, the script should fail with a clear error message.
- Keep the implementation portable where possible; if some metrics are unsupported on this OS, set them to null or leave lists empty while still producing the required files.
- Final artifacts expected: updated config/diagnostics.yaml, an implemented tools/session_diagnostics.py, external/king_in_yellow.txt, output/system_report.json, output/system_report.md, and output/email_draft_to_beta_readers.txt. All file paths are relative to the workspace directory.
